coredata.dc:title: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)-Diphenyl Carbazone (DPCO) loaded thin film sensors for fluorescent and colorimetric dual mode detection of mercury in various water resources
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Vyas, Tanmay
Joshi, Abhijeet
coredata.dc:description: Mitigation of the environmental pollution has attracted huge attention in recent years owing to the caused health hazards. Mercury ions are one of the toxic heavy metals as pollutants that cause severe damage to health and the environment. Fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection of mercury in water resources is gaining popularity globally due to ease of measurement and the possibility of onsite measurement. We herein report a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode Diphenyl carbazone (DPCO) and Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) thin film sensor for specific detection of mercury ions. The thin film sensors were characterized using various techniques like XPS, XRD, TEM, and CLSM. The colorimetric detection is based on the specific binding of DPCO with mercury ions forming a violet-colored complex with simultaneous production of protons. The intensity of color formation was quantified with a smartphone camera and the images using ImageJ software yielding a limit of detection (LOD) of 290 ppb. Fluorometric estimation was performed at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and an emission wavelength of 505 nm which was possible due to the pH sensitive nature of CQDs in the thin film. Mercury (Hg2 +) detection to an extent of 1.8 ppb was made possible in the linear range of 0–100 μM using fluorometric method with a response time of 2 min. Accuracy of estimation of Hg2 + detection was evaluated by spiking different water samples like Tap water, and two river water samples and resulted in the range of 100–103 %. Current investigation shows that DPCO-CQDs loaded thin films coupled with a fiber-optic device and smartphone imaging could be used for accurate dual mode sensing of Hg2 + in real water resource samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water pollution
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Diphenyl carbazone (DPCO)
Mercury
Fiber-optic device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods
Materials & instrumentation
Synthesis of PA-TED-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Development of CQDs and 1, 3-diphenyl carbazone (DPCO) loaded thin film sensors
Characterization of the CQDs-DPCO-loaded thin film sensors
Evaluation of pH response of CQDs-DPCO loaded thin films
Colorimetric estimation of mercury using-DPCO-CQD films
Fluorometric estimation of mercury heavy metal using CQDs-DPCO thin film sensors
Mercury spiking studies in various resources of water (household & river samples)
Specificity and stability studies of CQDs-DPCO loaded thin film sensor
Leaching and photostability of CQDs-DPCO thin film
Statistical analysis
Result and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs/DPCO-CQDs thin film
Evaluation of pH response of synthesized CQD-DPCO thin films
Colorimetric estimation of mercury ions using-DPCO-CQD sensor films
Fluorometric estimation of mercury heavy metal using CQDs-DPCO thin film sensors
Specificity studies of DPCO-CQDs thin film sensor
Spiking studies of mercury and validation of sensing performance using river water and household drinking water samples
Validation and comparison of analytical parameters of fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection of mercury ions
Photo-stability and leaching studies of CQDs-DPCO thin film
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the development and synthesis of CQDs-DPCO-based thin film sensor.

Characterization of thin film: A) TEM characterization of CQDs B) Absorbance of DPCO and CQDs, C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-DPCO thin film, D) XRD analysis of DPCO and CQDs-DPCO thin film, E) XPS analysis of CQDs-DPCO thin film F) CLSM analysis of CQDs-DPCO thin film.
Fluorescent response of DPCO-CQDs loaded thin film with different pH; A) Overlay spectra, B) Linear curve of pH sensing.
A) Colour response with different concentrations of mercury, B) RGB Intensity linear calibration curves analyzed on ImageJ software against different concentrations of mercury.
Mechanism of Colorimetric and Fluorometric dual mode sensing of the CQDs-DPCO thin film for the detection of Hg+2 ions.

A) Reaction of mercury ions with 1,5-Diphenyl carbazone, Calibration curve of CQDs-DPCO thin film fluorescence response with different concentrations of mercury ions; A) Overlay spectra, B) Linear curve.

Interference study of CQDs-DPCO chemo-sensor; A) Normalized intensity of different metals and ions at the same concentration produced via the Fluorometric method, B) Photographic image of colorimetric response of mercury ions in presence of DPCO compared to other interferences in water samples.

Spiking studies of CQDs thin film-based detection of mercury in various matrices of water resources, with fluorometric detection, A) Household Tap water, B) Narmada River water, C) Kshipra River water, and with colorimetric detection D) Household Tap water, E) Narmada River water, F) Kshipra River water.
A) Leaching studies of DPCO from CQDs-DPCO thin film, B) Photo-stability of CQDs-DPCO thin film.
Comparison of analytical parameters of fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection of mercury heavy metal ions.
Comparison of the detection limit of the present study compared to previous investigations.

head.ce:title: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)-Diphenyl Carbazone (DPCO) loaded thin film sensors for fluorescent and colorimetric dual mode detection of mercury in various water resources

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Khandwa Road, Indore, 453552, Madhya Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biosciences and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosciences and Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Khandwa Road, Indore- 453552, Madhya Pradesh, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Biosciences and Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Khandwa Road, Simrol, Indore, 453552, Madhya Pradesh, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biosciences and Biomedical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitigation of the environmental pollution has attracted huge attention in recent years owing to the caused health hazards. Mercury ions are one of the toxic heavy metals as pollutants that cause severe damage to health and the environment. Fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection of mercury in water resources is gaining popularity globally due to ease of measurement and the possibility of onsite measurement. We herein report a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode Diphenyl carbazone (DPCO) and Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) thin film sensor for specific detection of mercury ions. The thin film sensors were characterized using various techniques like XPS, XRD, TEM, and CLSM. The colorimetric detection is based on the specific binding of DPCO with mercury ions forming a violet-colored complex with simultaneous production of protons. The intensity of color formation was quantified with a smartphone camera and the images using ImageJ software yielding a limit of detection (LOD) of 290 ppb. Fluorometric estimation was performed at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and an emission wavelength of 505 nm which was possible due to the pH sensitive nature of CQDs in the thin film. Mercury (Hg2 +) detection to an extent of 1.8 ppb was made possible in the linear range of 0–100 μM using fluorometric method with a response time of 2 min. Accuracy of estimation of Hg2 + detection was evaluated by spiking different water samples like Tap water, and two river water samples and resulted in the range of 100–103 %. Current investigation shows that DPCO-CQDs loaded thin films coupled with a fiber-optic device and smartphone imaging could be used for accurate dual mode sensing of Hg2 + in real water resource samples.

CQDs-DPCO thin film-based sensor for the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of Hg2+ ions in various water resources.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The rapid developments in society, especially industrialization, have increased the pollution of the environment all over the globe. Water resources are the most affected part of the environment which is critical to be studied considering its prevalence and importance for human life [1]. Several pollutants cause water pollution such as heavy metals, pesticides, dyes, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) etc. [2]. Among them heavy metals are more hazardous due to their persistence in nature, bio-accumulation, and non-biodegradable properties [3] various heavy metals such as copper, nickel, cobalt, mercury, etc. can easily enter the human body via drinking water, food, and air [4]. Mercury is one of the toxic heavy metals and the main sources of mercury ions in soil and water are mainly from rocks, the atmosphere, caustic soda, mercury lamps, mining sites, coal plants, etc. [5]. The presence of mercury heavy metal ions in water resources causes severe toxic effects such as kidney failure, damage to the brain, skin damage, lung cancer, damage to biological membranes, Minamata disease etc [6–8]. The hazardous effects and toxicity of mercury ions make it significant to monitor and study their presence using a simple, quick, and specific detection method in various environmental resources.

Various classical techniques have been used for the detection of mercury such as Induced coupled plasma (ICP) [9], Atomic emission spectroscopy (AES), Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [10], Hydride generation atomic absorption spectrometry (HG-AAS) [11], Surface-enhanced Raman scattering [12] etc. All these techniques are well-known for their specificity and sensitivity as they are highly sophisticated instruments but have several disadvantages like high-cost instrumentation, non-portability, huge size, complex sample preparation, lack of on-site detection capability, etc. These disadvantages of the traditional methods indicate the requirement for the development of cost-effective, rapid, simple, and on-site detection method for mercury especially in water resources. Optical-based detection based on fluorometric, and colorimetric methods are one of the most advantageous techniques which fulfils all demerits of traditional methods. Simple and sensitive detection based on changes in fluorescence and color change can act as a game changer in mercury detection for on-site and resource poor settings. The changes in color or fluorescence intensity can be recorded by a smartphone to capture and analyze using software like Image J, Adobe Photoshop etc. Smartphone imaging and image analysis based quantification can act as efficient tools for easy, naked eye, and point-of-care detection of the important pollutants [13,14]. Various fluorometric along with colorimetric-based methods are used by researchers for environmental analytes detection like Dyes [15], Heavy metals [3], Picric acid [16], VOCs [17], Pesticides [18], etc. Fluorometric methods generally use fluorophores such as probes [19], metallic organic frames [20], quantum dots [21], organic dyes [22], etc. For example, the Imidazole-based fluorescent carbon dots were used to detect the nickel heavy metal ions based on the quenching mechanism with a detection limit of 0.93 mM [23]. The fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have various advantageous features over other fluorescence-based probes, like their non-toxicity, bio-tunability, biocompatibility, along with other favourable optical properties such as broad excitation, narrow emission, high stability with long fluorescence lifetime. A fluorescent dual-mode carbon nanodots probe was synthesized for the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of the mercury ions in water with a detection limit of 6.8 nM for fluorometric and 0.38 μM for colorimetric detection [24]. In another study a chemo-sensor comprised of 7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazolyl (NBD) with piperazine derivative was synthesized for the mercury detection based on the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism with the limit of detection of 19.2 nM [25] The carbon dots composed of the vinyl-functionalized polyphenolic group have been used to detect mercury ions through ionic interaction and fluorescence mechanism with a detection limit of 10.6 nM [26]. All these previous investigations can detect mercury with low detection limits but have disadvantages like complex synthesis procedures, use of fluorophores, lower sensitivity values, and higher response time. Such demerits need to be addressed for successful point of care detection system for toxic pollutants in complex environmental samples.

In the present investigation, Phthalic acid and Tri ethylene Diamine (TED) based CQDs and 1,5- diphenyl carbazone (DPCO) loaded thin film sensor was synthesized. The thin film sensors were characterized using several optical, morphological, elemental, and structural properties. The CQDs-DPCO loaded thin film-based sensor was utilized for the dual mode fluorometric and colorimetric detection of mercury-heavy metal ions. The results of colorimetric sensing on a smartphone were analyzed using Image J analysis software whereas results of fluorometric detection were obtained on a Fiber optic spectrometer coupled to a reflectance probe. Analytical parameters like accuracy, detection limit, response time, sensitivity, resolution, etc. were then validated for both the methods. Real water samples like two river water samples (Narmada River and Kshipra River) and household tap water were used for spiking studies to evaluate the suitability of the synthesized sensor for mercury detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials & instrumentation
ce:section.ce:para: Tri-ethylene diamine (MW 112.17 g/mol, TED), Chitosan (MW; 1526.5 g/mol), and Phthalic acid (MW 166.14 g/mol, PA), were procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. Mercury chloride (HgCl2) (MW; 271.50 g/mol) and 1,5- diphenyl carbazone (MW; 240.26 g/mol), were procured from the Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt Ltd (SRL) India. Double distilled water was used for the preparation of all buffers, working solutions; carbon quantum dots (CQDs), etc. The Fiber optic spectrometer of Ocean Optics, USA used for fluorescence-based detection of mercury comprised of a detector that has specification of FLAME-XR1-ES-SONY ILX511b detector, a 10.08 mW output light source of 365 nm UV light source (LED Light Source LSM- 365A, Ocean Insights) and SMA905 fiber connector connected with the optical cable with 600 μm*2 m dimension which was connected to the Reflectance probe with the spectrum range of 200–1025 nm (RP 22, 200 μM, 0.22 NA) Thor Labs, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PA-TED-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: PA (2 M) and TED (2.9 M) were mixed in an equal volume in 10 mL of the MilliQ water for the preparation of CQDs as per a protocol described by Choudhary et al. [27]. The mixed solution of PA and TED was kept in a microwave oven (Samsung, Malaysia), for 2 min at 700 W. After the microwave exposure, the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature and filtered through a 0.2 μm sized membrane filter and subsequently dialysed through a membrane having a molecular cut-off of 14 kDa in double distilled water. Finally, the filtered and dialyzed solution of the CQDs synthesized was lyophilized to get powdered CQDs. The powdered CQDs were stored in refrigerated conditions until used further.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of CQDs and 1, 3-diphenyl carbazone (DPCO) loaded thin film sensors
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs suspension is mixed with the 10 % w/v DPCO (dissolved in acetone) and the mixture of CQDs-DPCO solution is mixed into the 5 % w/v of the chitosan polymer solution. The CQD-DPCO suspension was solvent casted in petri plates. The solvent was evaporated by keeping the films overnight for air drying to obtain the dried CQDs-DPCO thin film sensors. The CQDs-DPCO loaded chitosan thin film was cut into the smaller circular discs through the paper punch cutter having an approximate size of 5 mm. The discs were stored at room temperature until used for the characterization and sensing studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CQDs-DPCO-loaded thin film sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-DPCO-loaded thin film sensors were characterized by various techniques. The optical characterization of CQDs loaded thin film was done by measuring the absorbance of the DPCO and CQDs solution with a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV1900i, Shimadzu, Japan). The measurement of the fluorescence of the bare CQDs and CQDs-DPCO solution was done through the fiber optic spectrometer (FOS) device (Ocean Insight, USA). The morphological and size distribution characteristics of synthesized CQDs were performed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (CM 20, Philips, India). For this, CQDs were drop casted on a copper grid having a size of 400 μm, for the TEM analysis. X-ray diffraction (XRD), (Rigaku, Japan) was used to analyze the crystalline and lattice parameters of CQDs-DPCO thin film with the Cu X-ray (1.54 Å). For this DPCO and CQD were freeze dried and then subjected to XRD measurement. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Beamline PES-BL-14, India) was used to analyze the chemical and composition characteristics of bare DPCO and CQDs-DPCO thin film to validate the composition of the synthesized thin film with standard compositional properties. In the CQDs loaded thin film, the CQDs distribution was analyzed by the Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM), (IX83, Olympus, Japan) with filter setting of bandwidths of 500–700 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of pH response of CQDs-DPCO loaded thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The response of CQDs-DPCO loaded thin film was evaluated with the different pH buffers from pH 3 to pH 9. The CQDs-DPCO loaded thin film was dipped into the different pH buffers from pH 3 to pH 9 and the fluorescence was evaluated through the FOS device with an excitation at 365 nm & 505 nm emission wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric estimation of mercury using-DPCO-CQD films
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized thin film was dipped into the solution containing mercury, the DPCO present in the thin film was able to form a complex with mercury and form a violet color complex and this violet color complex was imaged using a smartphone camera, the image analysis was done by using the ImageJ software (NIH, USA; http://rsb.info.nih.gov/ij). RGB color intensities were quantified using this software and correlated with the concentrations of mercury to develop a calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric estimation of mercury heavy metal using CQDs-DPCO thin film sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorometric evaluation of synthesized CQDs-DPCO thin film is performed by dipping the CQDs-DPCO thin film into the mercury-containing solution of different concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM. The DPCO from the thin film will form a violet color complex with mercury along with the production of proton ions, which causes a change in the pH of the solution. The pH change of the solution is detected by CQDs embedded in the thin film. The synthesized thin film was dipped into each concentration of mercury solution and incubated for 2 min and due to pH change the fluorescence of the CQDs of the respective solution was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and emission wavelength of 505 nm. The calibration curve was prepared between the fluorescence obtained from fiber optic device and concentrations of the mercury. The fluorescence from the films was captured by the reflectance probe which transfers the signals to the fiber optic spectrometer detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mercury spiking studies in various resources of water (household & river samples)
ce:section.ce:para: Mercury spiking and recovery studies in various water resources were done to check the suitability of the synthesized fluorometric and colorimetric sensors in complex real water samples. For the spiking studies along with household tap water, river samples were taken from the Kshipra River (Ujjain, India), and the Narmada River (Omkareshwar, Khandwa, India). The CQDs-DPCO thin film was dipped into the various concentrations of mercury (0–100 μM) containing real water samples and fluorescence emission was recorded. The calibration curves were used for the calculation of the predicted/estimated concentration of spiked mercury in various real water samples. The accuracy of estimation of mercury ions in real samples was represented as % recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability studies of CQDs-DPCO loaded thin film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the sensing assay for the mercury detection was evaluated by exposing the CQDs-DPCO thin film to other heavy metal ions such as lead, nickel, copper, and cobalt, and ions that are typically present in water such as calcium, potassium, magnesium, and sodium etc. at a concentration of 100 μM. Fluorescence emission was measured post-exposure of interferents which were measured using a FOS device. Colorimetric signals were measured using smartphone camera which was later analyzed through Image J-based calculation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Leaching and photostability of CQDs-DPCO thin film
ce:section.ce:para: In the leaching study of CQDs -DPCO thin film, the thin film was dipped into water for several cycles and the absorbance of solution was measured using the UV–Vis spectrometer. The photo-stability of CQDs-loaded thin film was analyzed by the FOS device over 100 days. The dried films were excited with the 365 nm light and emission was captured using a reflectance probe attached to the FOS device. The signals were used to evaluate the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs loaded thin films at regular intervals over 100 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of the significance of the experimental data in the student paired t-test a Pearson correlation coefficient (r.). The t-test was calculated to evaluate whether the means of the 2 groups were equal or not (real and estimated groups). The R-value significance determines the actual value and shows a high similarity and significance above 0.95.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Result and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs/DPCO-CQDs thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The PA-TED CQDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method as described in the previously published report [3] (Fig. 1 ). Briefly, pyrolysis of the PA and TED were allowed to happen in anaerobic conditions in a microwave. TED acts as a passivant for PA CQDs. The quantum yield of the CQDs was found to be enhanced by using the long-chain aromatic compound PA, along with the addition of TED acting as a passivant. The amine structure of TED is also responsible for enhancing the CQDs quantum yield and providing long wavelength emission. CQDs were purified by filtration and dialysis for the preparation of CQDs thin films by solvent casting. Even in the immobilized form, the CQD retained all their properties. Morphological characteristics as evaluated by TEM showed a monodisperse particle population with a size in the range of 10 ± 2 nm (Fig. 2 A). Chitosan was used as an immobilization polymer considering it has a biodegradable nature to prevent any adverse effects due to the carrier or any issue related to deposition. PA-TED CQDs have a pH-sensitive nature which was confirmed as per previously published reports. DPCO on the other hand acts as an analyte-specific probe that can interact with Hg2 + ions and provide a colorimetric contrast in the medium. DPCO shows an absorbance at λmax of 200 nm along with some absorption at 340 nm as described in Fig. 2B. The spectrum of CQD was confirmed to have absorption maxima at 365 nm as per previously published reports. The CQDs can be excited using a single light source of 365 nm and produce fluorescence emission. This indicates that the DPCO does not interfere with CQD thin film fluorescence. The optical characterization and the analysis of absorption pattern of the synthesized CQDs was done by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1900i, Shimadzu, Japan). The maximum absorption was observed at λmax = 365 nm mentioned in Fig. 2B. The emission of the synthesized CQDs thin film and CQD-DPCO thin films was done by using fiber optic spectrometer (FOS) device and the emission was observed at 505 nm (Fig. 2C) at room temperature and neutral pH conditions. The thin film development and DPCO co-immobilization do not alter the fluorescence emission signal of CQDs. The characteristic peaks at 20–28 °2θ were observed in XRD spectra [27]. The XRD peak of DPCO was also found to be around 18 °2θ, as shown in Fig. 2D. DPCO does not show the peak in XRD due to its low concentration compared to the CQDs and probable amorphization of DPCO while being solvent casted. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CQDs thin film, was performed to evaluate the lattice parameters and crystalline characterization. The analysis indicates the prominent peak of 22.5°, which indicates the plane (002), with 0.65 d-spacing value. The chemical composition of the CQDs thin films was analyzed with the XPS and the prominent peaks were observed at 297 eV for the C1s, 530 eV for the O1s, and a slight peak of N1s at 403 eV. The presence of DPCO in thin film showed the presence of only 3 elements carbon, oxygen and nitrogen confirming of the presence of C–C, C–O–H, CC, and CO bonds as shown in Fig. 2E. The XPS analysis was found to be similar to the previous investigations for CQDs characterization [3]. The evaluation of CQDs fluorophores in thin film was evaluated by the CLSM (Fig. 2F). It was observed that CQDs loaded thin film has an even and uniform distribution of CQDs fluorophores throughout the thin film. The dimension of DPCO – CQDs thin film was found to be 5 mm and the amount of DPCO is around 0.75 mg in the single thin film of CQDs-DPCO thin film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of pH response of synthesized CQD-DPCO thin films
ce:section.ce:para: Responsiveness of fluorescence against pH changes in the range of pH 3 to pH 10 was determined by pH sensitive nature of the CQDs. The FOS device response suggested an increase in fluorescence intensity with an increase in pH as described in Fig. 3 . The production of the protons (H+) causes a drop in pH and consequently leads to the withdrawal of electrons from the CQDs leading to quenching of fluorescence. Similarly, when the pH of the solution increases towards the basicity due to higher OH− ions, an increase in fluorescence emission was observed. The pH response of CQDs- DPCO was found to be maintained and was found to be similar to the bare CQDs described in previous reports.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric estimation of mercury ions using-DPCO-CQD sensor films
ce:section.ce:para: The colored reaction of mercury with DPCO forming the violet-blue complex can be visualized through the naked eye for different concentrations of mercury ions in Fig. 4 . However, quantifying the color produced needs the use of instrument like a UV–Visible spectrometer. An alternative method was investigated where in the formation of a colored complex provoked the utilization of RGB intensity for the quantification. Commercially available ImageJ software (NIH, USA; http://rsb.info.nih.gov/ij) was used for color measurement of the digital images of the chemical reaction clicked through a mobile camera (Redmi Note 10 pro 64 MP). As the concentration of the mercury increased, the more intense violet-blue color produced changes in the pixel intensity of the photographic images. The mercury concentration in the water was found to be directly proportional to the blue color intensity. The intensity of blue color as quantified from ImageJ was found to be linearly increasing with increasing mercury ion concentrations in the solution and the red and green color intensity were found to decrease. The regression equation of the calibration curve between the blue color intensity and concentration of mercury ions as analytes was found to be y = 0.019x+1.05 as shown in Fig. 4. The sensitivity was calculated and found to be 0.019 units/μM of mercury heavy metal along the regression coefficient (R2) is 0.9911. The sensing analytical parameters were calculated from the calibration curve and the detection limit with this method was observed to be 0.297 ppm along with a resolution of 0.09 ppm.

The optimum conditions for the reaction of sensing assay were observed concerning response time and temperature as shown in Fig. S2. It was observed that the reaction of sensing assay with mercury ions was observed to be in equilibrium at 2 min (Fig. S2A). Further, upon studying the effect of temperature the response were found to be stable from 25 °C to 40 °C as described in Fig. S2B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric estimation of mercury heavy metal using CQDs-DPCO thin film sensors
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are known for quenching processes with many inorganic heavy metal ions. Heavy metal ions lead to quenching of CQDs fluorescence. However, to detect mercury specifically a chemical probe was used. DPCO is a specific probe for Hg2 + forming a violet complex with the production of the protons (H+) ions as mentioned in the mechanism of Fig. 5 . As the concentration of the mercury ions is increased a greater concentration of DPCO-Hg complex will be caused and will cause liberation of more protons. The protons will thus cause a pH change in the microenvironment of the films. The change in the pH will be detected by the pH sensitive CQDs in the films which is recorded at 505 nm when excited at 365 nm in the FOS device.

The calibration curve of mercury ion concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM with DPCO-CQDs thin film showed a linearly decreasing correlation with a rise in mercury concentration. The protons produced from the complexation reaction cause a drop in the fluorescence emission from the CQDs. The regression equation of the calibration curve between the CQDs thin film fluorescence response and concentration of mercury ions as analytes was found to be linear with equation following y = 0.0068x+1.001 as mentioned in Fig. 6 . The sensitivity was found to be 0.0068 unit changes/μM of the mercury heavy metal ions with a regression coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The detection limit of 1.8 ppb was observed which covers the permissible limit range and is far lower as compared to colorimetric detection. A corresponding drop in pH was also observed from pH 6.5-pH 4 when a concentration change from 0 to 100 μM was occurring.

The calibration curve estimation of different mercury ion concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM with DPCO-CQDs thin film was done in different pH buffers that is pH 4 and pH 8 buffer conditions as mentioned in Fig. S1 A, B with a vision to detect the mercury ion in real water samples having different pH values. The comparison of calibration curves of mercury ion concentrations with different pH buffer conditions was found to behave in a similar manner as mentioned in Fig. S1 C. This clearly indicates that the synthesized chemo-sensor can detect the mercury heavy metal ions in real water samples. The mercury detection through the synthesized sensor shows a prediction of the value of the regression coefficient is 0.99 with the P(tstat<tcritical)>0.05. Statistical data analysis suggests that the null hypothesis in which equal means of any two groups could not be rejected. The student-paired t-test and r value indicate the suitability of the sensor based on the CQDs- DPCO-based thin film for the detection of mercury as a point-of-care sensor to a great extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity studies of DPCO-CQDs thin film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the sensing assay based on the DPCO-CQDs thin film sensor, the sensor was used to detect other heavy metals ions such as cobalt, copper, lead, and nickel and common ions typically present in water samples as mentioned in Fig. 7 A and B. The results indicate that naked-eye detection is possible for mercury and no color complex was formed with other heavy metals or other ions. Similarly, in the case of fluorometric estimation, no protons are produced and no fluorescence change occurs in the pH-sensitive CQDs loaded in thin film with other interferent. It was observed that only the mercury-DPCO complex can produce a violet color complex and subsequently producing the protons to give fluorescence emission changes. It indicates that DPCO-CQDs thin film-based sensor can specifically detect the mercury ions without any interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spiking studies of mercury and validation of sensing performance using river water and household drinking water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Spiking studies were done to evaluate the suitability of the fluorescence of CQDs thin film and determine the interference present in the real water resources for the sensing of mercury. The river water samples from Narmada River, Kshipra River and household tap water are used for the spiking various concentrations of mercury and evaluated using DPCO-CQDs thin film-based fluorometric and colorimetric detection. The accuracy value in terms of the percentage recovery of the spiking studies was found to be enhanced as compared to the previous investigations. In the household water sample, the accuracy in terms of percentage recovery was observed to be 101.2 % (Fig. 8 A). The percentage recovery in terms of accuracy shows excellent suitability for the detection of mercury with CQDs-DPCO thin film-based fluorometric detection without any interference. Considering the fact that household water samples may be treated and purified through several processes, so real samples from river water samples (Narmada and Kshipra) were evaluated for the spiking studies. In the case of the Narmada River water samples, the percentage recovery in terms of accuracy was found to be 101.1 % (Fig. 8B). In a similar fashion in the Kshipra River, the percentage recovery was 101.4 % (Fig. 8C). Overall estimation error ranged from 1.2 to 1.4 % in all three real water samples which indicates a great suitability of the thin film sensors for quantification of Hg2 + ions. Similarly, the colorimetric-based detection shows the accuracy in terms of percentage recovery in tap/household water was observed to be 101.23 % with a mean error of 1.23 % (Fig. 8D) while in both river water, the accuracy was found to be in Narmada (100.25 ± 0.25 %) (Fig. 8E) & Kshipra (101.68 ± 1.68 %) (Fig. 8F) (Table 1 ). In all water resources (Tap, Narmada, and Kshipra) samples, the spiking studies indicated that CQDs-DPCO thin film-based sensor with fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection shows good accuracy around 100 % for the detection of mercury. The accuracy of all three real water matrices was also performed with the different pH buffers such as pH 4 and pH 8 and it was found comparable accuracy in terms of percentage recovery as found in comparison to neutral pH 7 as mentioned in Table S1. The accuracy indicates that the DPCO-CQDs thin film has tremendous suitability for the detection of mercury even in complex real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation and comparison of analytical parameters of fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection of mercury ions
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical parameters of detection such as sensitivity, linearity, resolution, LOD, and Limit of Quantification (LOQ) of both fluorometric and colorimetric-based detection are described in Table 1. The linearity in terms of the regression coefficient was found to be 0.99 for fluorometric and 0.98 for colorimetric which indicates a good linear correlation, both methods have shown linearity for quantification of results. The sensitivity of the sensing mechanism was found 2.8 fold higher in the case of the colorimetric method as compared to the fluorometric method. However, the colorimetric method uses a smartphone imaging and digital image analysis platform compared to the spectroscopic measurements of UV–Visible machine. The accuracy determined in terms of the percentage recovery was found to be similar (near 100 %) in both methods for the detection of mercury. The resolution, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantification (LOQ) were found to be 16.1 times superior in case of fluorometric-based detection as compared to the colorimetric methods. Thus, it indicates the suitability of both methods for estimation of mercury considering the permissible limit of mercury 0.04 ppm in water samples. All the analytical parameters show excellent values, making the synthesized thin film sensor for the detection of mercury in various water matrices superior in comparison to previous literature reports (Table 2 ). Further, the sensing method was found to be suitable for real-time monitoring considering the fiber optic fluorescence estimation and smartphone based colorimetric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-stability and leaching studies of CQDs-DPCO thin film
ce:section.ce:para: In the leaching study of CQDs -DPCO thin film, the thin film was dipped into water for up to 10 cycles and the absorbance was measured. It was observed that 50 % of the DPCO were leached out from the thin film in the 6th cycle as mentioned in Fig. 9 A and able to react with mercury in water matrices. The leaching pattern suggests that individual thin films can be useful as disposable sensors for mercury ions. The photo-stability of CQDs-DPCO loaded thin film indicates great photo-stability for over 100 days with only about a 3–4% drop in fluorescence intensity and minimum photo-bleaching (Fig. 9B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The present study demonstrates the application of DPCO-CQDs thin film-based chemical sensors for the specific fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of mercury ions in several water resources. The DPCO conjugated on the CQDs loaded thin film forms a specific violet color complex with mercury which can be used as a mode for colorimetric detection accompanied by Smartphone imaging and digital image analysis. On the other hand fluorometric method can is useful alternative with a fiber optic device using the pH sensitive properties of CQDs. pH based mercury detection using DPCO-CQDs was found to be working in the real water resource samples. Mercury could be detected with a better detection limit of 1.8 ppb and 297 ppb respectively in the concentration range of 0–100 μM concentration with good sensitivity of 0.0068/μM and 0.019/μM, respectively of mercury heavy metal ions. The LOD of fluorometric-based detection was about 15 times better than that colorimetric-based detection. The accuracy in terms of average percentage recovery was also found to be near 100 % in both colorimetric and fluorometric detection. The result shows the synthesized DPCO-CQDs loaded thin film-based sensor is excellent candidate for the dual mode colorimetric and fluorometric-based specific detection of mercury ions. These methods also support the point-of-care approach and possibilities of real-time monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Tanmay Vyas: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Abhijeet Joshi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: TV acknowledges financial support from the Prime Minister Research Fellowship (PMRF) 2101309, & AJ acknowledges Department of Biotechnology India (BT/PR24652/NER/795/2017).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115700.
